title: Fish Pie

overview.description: A classic British fish pie with mixed fish in a creamy leek and parsley sauce topped with fluffy mashed potato.

ingredients: 700g mixed fish, cut into large pieces
method: Preheat the oven to 200°C.
Place the potatoes in a large saucepan of salted water.
Bring to the boil and cook until completely tender.
Drain thoroughly and allow the potatoes to steam dry briefly.
Mash with the butter and milk until smooth.
Season with salt and black pepper.
Melt the butter in a large saucepan over medium heat.
Add the leek and cook gently for 8–10 minutes until softened.
Add the flour and stir for 1–2 minutes.
Gradually add the milk, stirring constantly to make a smooth sauce.
Add the cream and Dijon mustard.
Simmer gently until the sauce thickens.
Remove from the heat.
Stir in the parsley, lemon zest, lemon juice and peas.
Season to taste.
Add the raw fish and prawns to a large ovenproof dish.
Pour over the hot sauce.
Allow to stand for a few minutes so the fish begins to warm through.
Spread the mashed potato over the top.
Rough up the surface with a fork.
Scatter over the cheddar if using.
Bake for 30–40 minutes until bubbling and golden on top.
Allow to rest for 5–10 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Fish pie is a British comfort food that developed from the tradition of combining fish with a thick sauce and a pastry or potato topping.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a smooth white sauce and how residual heat and oven cooking gently cook delicate fish without drying it out.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A comforting family meal that makes excellent use of frozen fish and can be assembled ahead or batch-cooked for the freezer.
chapman-tips: Use a mixture of fish for the best texture and flavour.
Firm white fish provides structure while salmon adds richness and colour.
Don't overcook the fish before assembling; it will cook in the sauce and oven.
Let the mashed potatoes steam dry after boiling so the topping does not become watery.
Rough up the potato surface with a fork to create crispy peaks.
The sauce should be fairly thick before the fish is added.
Don't overfill the dish; leave room for the sauce to bubble.
A little lemon helps balance the richness of the sauce.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Assemble and cool completely before freezing, then thaw thoroughly before baking where practical.
notes.leftovers: Reheats well and can be portioned for lunches.
